observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that Management:

a. Require the Inventory Committee, General Services Office (GSO) and the
Municipal Accounting Office to prioritize the reconciliation process of
their respective records and avail the one-time cleansing of PPE account
balances, strictly in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 and
ensure that discrepancies noted are immediately investigated and adjusted
in the books accordingly;

b. Require the Municipal Accounting Office concerned personnel to maintain
complete and updated PPELCs to support GL account balances and the
GSO concerned personnel to keep corresponding Property Cards for
control purposes;

c. Direct the Municipal Accountant to continue tracing back prior years’
acquisitions of PPE to have a complete detail of the PPE accounts so that
the corresponding depreciation could be computed, to present fairly the
carrying value of the depreciable PPE; and

d. Continue exerting efforts in completing the physical count and in having a
complete and accurate RPCPPE in relation to the deficiencies noted.

We recommended that Management:

a. Require the Municipal Accountant to:

i. Prepare and submit the BRS on a monthly basis, together with the
copies of the bank statements and supporting documents for the
reconciling items, within the prescribed period for a timely
review/verification of such report;

ii. Conduct review on the variances noted between the GL and Bank
Statements balances and prepare necessary adjusting entries, if
warranted;

b. Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to maintain and update the Cash in Bank
cashbooks for all accounts regularly; and

c. Direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to review, analyze
and reconcile their records to determine the nature of each account and
come up with the correct balances of the Cash in Bank accounts for all
funds.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer and all
Revenue Collectors/Accountable Officers to immediately deposit/remit the balance of
the entire undeposited collections to the AGDB. Also, desist from handling huge
amount of cash in their possession and deposit all their collections in accordance with
the aforementioned regulations, thus, discouraging them from using their collections
for personal or other purposes.

We further recommended to exert effort to collect the accountabilities of previous
Accountable Officers who have already retired. Henceforth, avoid giving clearance to
retiring municipal employees unless all their accountabilities with the LGU are fully
settled.

We recommended that Management:

a. Require all AOs to settle their respective CAs at the end of the year to
reflect the correct balances of the accounts affected in the books of
accounts of the Agency; and

b. Henceforth, avoid granting subsequent CAs to AOs with outstanding CAs
and withhold the salaries and/or any amount due those AOs who were
unable to comply with the pertinent rules and regulations on CAs in
accordance with the afore-cited law and regulations.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to:

a. Strictly monitor the conduct of regular and periodic verification, analysis
and validation of the existence of payable accounts to ensure that only
valid payables are recorded in the books; and

b. Prepare a certified schedule of aging of dormant payable accounts for
which there are no valid claimants/creditors and no supporting
documents, afterwhich, revert the verified amounts to the Unappropriated
Surplus of the Government Equity Account by preparing the necessary
Journal Entry Vouchers as basis for recording in the books of accounts of
the Municipality.

We recommended that Management:

a. Ensure that the Municipal Accountant and OIC-Municipal Treasurer
immediately release the amount due to various barangays of the
Municipality;

b. Direct the Municipal Accountant to maintain records and SL for each
barangay for the current and previous years; and

c. Require them to adhere strictly to the guidelines relative to the prompt
release of the barangay shares from realty taxes stated under Section 271 (d)
of the Local Government Code in the ensuing years.

We recommended that Management:

a. Require the BAC/GSO to submit to the Audit Team copies of the perfected
contracts on the procurement of infrastructure projects with complete
supporting documents within five working days from execution of contract
for the auditorial and technical review and evaluation, in accordance with
the aforecited guidelines;

b. Direct the BAC and Municipal Accountant to submit the lacking documents;
and

c. Require the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all payments related
thereto are supported with complete documentation conformably with the
aforecited laws, rules, and regulations.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the MDRRM Council
through the MDRRMO to revisit the guidelines in the appropriation and utilization
of the MDRRMF. Likewise, refrain from charging non-priority programs in the
MDRRMF and ensure that the MDRRMFIP does not include administrative
expenses instead prioritize appropriation for the procurement of equipment useful
for disaster prevention, preparedness, response, rehabilitation and recovery.

Further, require the MDRRMO to submit justification with legal basis to support
disbursements of MDRRMF amounting to ₱8,971,260.66 which were
inappropriately charged against the said fund.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the MDRRM Officer
and the Human Resource Management (HRM) Officer to initiate the creation of
additional one position in the LDRRMO and filling it up with appropriate and well-
trained personnel to properly institutionalize the organization of LDRRMO as
prescribed under NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC JMC No. 2014-1.

We recommended that Management:

a. Require the concerned local officials to submit the required reports namely:
supplemental LDIP and AIP and Quarterly Report on Fund Utilization and
Status of Program/Project Implementation in accordance with DBM LBC No.
154;

b. Consider issuing Certificate of Acceptance one year after completion of the
project to compel the contractors to undertake any repairs of the project
within the defects liability period in accordance and compliance with RA No.
9184;

c. Submit the lacking documentary requirements and explanation of the noted
deficiencies; and

d. Henceforth, indicate the actual duration of the project accomplishment in
each billing, and ensure that the actual date of project completion is properly
reflected in the certificate of completion, in compliance with the regulations.

We recommended that Management:

a. Require the HRMO to formulate a uniform policy and procedures in the
engagement of JO workers to ensure that the hiring process is free from
bias and has a consistent basis for assigning wage rates to improve the
existing system;

b. Direct the HRMO, Accounting Unit, and concerned departments to carefully
check/examine the submitted DTRs vis-à-vis ARs before preparation of
payroll and payment is made to avoid the same deficiency in the future;

c. Stop the practice of paying the salaries of JO personnel in advance or
before the end of the month; and

d. Submit justification on the noted deficiencies.

We recommended that that the Honorable Mayor:

a. Direct the Municipal Treasurer/Municipal Accountant to immediately submit
the lacking DVs for CY 2024 with complete supporting documents;

b. Institute appropriate disciplinary actions against those officials and employees
who are responsible for the delayed submission of monthly financial
reports, paid DVs, and payrolls; and

c. Require the officials concerned to comply with the rules and regulations on
the submission of financial reports, and paid DVs, ORs, and Payrolls.

We recommended that Management:

a. Prepare the GAD Agenda to align the PPAs to the administration’s plan with
respect to the implementation of GAD mandates and to ensure the promotion
of gender equality and empowerment of involved parties; and

b. Comply with the provisions of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2016-
01 by using the prescribed assessment tools such as GeRL tool in the
preparation of the GPB to ensure the gender responsiveness of the LGU’s
planned PPAs.

We recommended that Management:

a. Consider issuing Certificate of Acceptance one year after completion of the
project to compel the contractors to undertake any repairs of the project
within the defects liability period in accordance and compliance with RA
No. 9184; and

b. Henceforth, the remaining 50 percent Retention Money must be released at
the expiration of the defects liability period, which is one year from date of
completion or require the contractor to provide a bank guarantee equivalent
to the same amount, as required by the regulation.

We recommended proper management of the Municipality’s bank accounts to
avoid incurrence of unnecessary bank charges. We further recommended that
Management close the PRDP I-REAP Loan Proceeds and GOP bank accounts if
they will no longer be used to avoid further loss of government funds.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to
submit the monthly financial reports, paid DVs, and JEVs together with its
supporting documents within the prescribed period. Henceforth, strictly adhere to
the required period of submission of financial reports and their supporting
documents.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Engineer to provide the Inventory Committee with complete description and cost segregation of road components for road projects to facilitate the preparation of RPCLRN;
Direct the Inventory Committee and GSO to prepare and submit the RPCLRN and RLRN, respectively, in accordance with the prescribed formats as provided under COA Circular No. 2015-008; and
Require the Municipal Accountant to disclose the total road network in the Notes to the FS in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2015-008.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the GSO to prepare and maintain separate subsidiary records/LRNLCs and LRNPCs, respectively, for local road projects to ensure proper recording and control of the assets.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accounting Office and the Municipal Engineering Office to reconcile their records as to the status and costs incurred related to the ongoing and completed projects of the Municipality. Thereafter, require the Municipal Accountant to close the CIP accounts (Item Nos. 5 and 6) to its appropriate asset accounts and make proper disclosures in the Notes to FS of the circumstances of the balances.

We recommended that Management:
Require the Municipal Accountant to:
i) Prepare and submit the BRS on a monthly basis, together with the copies of the bank statements and supporting documents for the reconciling items, within the prescribed period for a timely review/verification of such report; ii) Conduct review on the variances noted between the GL and Bank Statements balances and prepare necessary adjusting entries, if warranted; and iii) Prepare and maintain Subsidiary Ledgers (SLs) of all bank accounts maintained by the agency for better monitoring of the movement of Cash in Bank, LCCA balances;
Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to maintain and update the Cash in Bank cashbooks for all accounts regularly; and
Direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to review, analyze and reconcile their records to determine the nature of each account and come up with the correct balances of the Cash in Bank accounts for all funds.

We recommended that Management:
Submit to the Audit Team copies of the MOA and supporting documents on the subject fund transfers not yet submitted and ensure their submission for any subsequent fund transfers; and
Direct the Accountant to:
Review, correct and update the identified SLs with negative balances and conduct periodical check for negative abnormal balances in the accounts and adjust accordingly;
Prepare adjusting entries to correct entries made in taking up LGU counterpart as Due to NGA;
Evaluate the non-moving SL balances and facilitate the necessary liquidation/refund of unexpended balances of those projects to the Source Agency; and
To submit to the concerned Source Agency the liquidation reports and return/remit the outstanding balance, for completed projects, if warranted.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant trace and analyze the pertinent transactions of the Intra-Agency accounts Due from/Due to Other Funds, reconcile the difference/s and prepare adjusting entry/ies, as necessary.

We recommended that Management strictly comply with DILG, DOF and DBM JMC No. 1, as follows:
Maximize the utilization of the 20 percent DF and implement the PPAs within the targeted timeframe to achieve the social, economic and environmental outcomes essential in the attainment of the constitutional objective of a desired quality of life for all;
Reprogram and reallocate resources as soon as the need arises to ensure that the Municipality is maximizing its available resources to achieve the expected results it has committed; and
Formulate well-defined procedures to enhance the monitoring and evaluation activities during project implementation, which includes among others, the coordination with the Municipal Engineer in monitoring the implementation of projects funded from the 20 percent DF to attain the optimal socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes which is the core of the fund allocation.

We recommended that Management require the LSB to prepare the LSB three-year program and to consider the recommendation of the DepEd PSDS and SDS as well as the required SIPs and DEDPs and other information from DepEd RO in the preparation of the SEF Budget. Henceforth, the LSB must strictly follow the mandated required processes and documents in planning and budgeting for SEF under the revised guidelines on the use of SEF pursuant to the above-mentioned JC.
Likewise, we recommended that the LSB strictly comply with the requirements of the said JC in identifying the PPAs that reflect an equitable distribution of the mandated priority expenditures and refrain from using the SEF outside the scope for which it was established.

We recommended that the Agency Head instruct the Municipal Accountant to submit the lacking CY 2023 DVs with complete supporting documents, collections accounts and necessary monthly reports within the reglementary period in accordance with Section 107 of PD No. 1445, Section 7.01 of COA Circular No. 95-006, Sections 7.1 and 7.2.1 of COA Circular No. 2009-006 and other pertinent laws, rules and regulations. Otherwise, the subject disbursements will be disallowed in audit.

We recommended that Management:
Strictly adhere to the above cited provisions on the procurement of infrastructure projects. Henceforth, all perfected contracts together with its supporting documents must be submitted to the Office of the Auditor within five working days from execution of contract for the required auditorial review and technical evaluation; and
Direct concerned officials to submit lacking documents and the BAC Secretariat to maintain a checklist of required documents to be submitted and ensure that contracts for infrastructure projects is properly supported with complete documentation.

We recommended that Management:
Require the concerned officials to submit the required lacking supporting documents in accordance with Section 4 (6) of PD No. 1445 and other related procurement guidelines; and
Submit explanation/justification on the discrepancies noted.

We recommended that Management require:
The Property Officer to prepare and submit the required PIF which contains the listing of all insurable properties and other assets of the agency to the Audit Team and of the GSIS GIF; and
The Official concerned to ensure that all insurable properties and other assets are adequately covered/insured with the GIF, administered by the GSIS to adequately and reliably protect against any damage to or loss of its properties or assets and interest due to fortuitous events/casualty.

We recommended that Management:
Inform the Audit Team within 10 days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the program/activity that the appropriate project signboards and/or public notices are already posted for the validation of the Audit Team; and
Require the Municipal Engineer to provide the Audit Team, at the beginning of the year, with a list of all on-going government PPAs and those that are to be implemented during the year including the required data/information in accordance with Section 2 of COA Circular No. 2013-004.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the GFPS to fully implement the GAD PPAs and monitor their implementation to ensure that optimum benefits are derived by its constituents;
Instruct the MPDC to facilitate the gathering of data and information needed for the creation of an updated and well-established GAD database to have an accurate and reliable basis in the planning and programming of PPAs as well as in the policy formulation in order to properly address the existing and emerging GAD issues and concerns; and
Direct the GFPS and GAD key personnel to undergo capacity building programs and training on gender and development to enhance their knowledge and build their competencies on GAD-related laws and international commitments, gender mainstreaming, gender analysis, gender-responsive planning and budgeting, and GAD-related tools like HGDG on the attributed budget to evaluate gender responsiveness of PPAs in the GAD Plan and Budget.

We recommended that Management require the concerned officials to submit the required lacking supporting documents in accordance with Section 4 (6) of PD No. 1445 and other related procurement guidelines.

We recommended that Management require the PG to recreate/reproduce the reports submitted to RPCO and immediately submit CYs 2020 to 2023 Fund Utilization Reports and other reports for monitoring of the Enterprise’s operations and appropriate recording of liquidations and closing of the PRDP I-REAP books.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to transfer fund from I-REAP LGU-Equity account to GF for the corresponding amount used for the purchases of Machinery and Equipment and Supplies and Materials intended for I-REAP Subproject. However, since the remaining Cash in Bank balance is below the required transfer, other I-REAP bank accounts can be utilized for this purpose. But, if the Agency intends to utilize these I-REAP bank accounts for future use, only the amount in excess of the ₱10,000.00 required maintaining balance must be transferred to avoid incurring unnecessary bank charges, otherwise close the accounts and refund the remaining I-REAP Cash in Bank balances to GF.

We recommended that Management replenish the PRDP I-REAP Loan Proceeds and GOP bank accounts if these accounts are still to be used in the succeeding fund releases to avoid incurring unnecessary bank charges and exercise due diligence in ensuring that there would be no loss or wastage of government funds, otherwise close the said bank accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant observe due diligence in recording transactions by using the appropriate accounts under COA Circular No. 2015-009 and following the series of proforma entries in taking up all transactions on RPT and SET and to reconcile the affected accounts and effect the necessary adjusting/correcting entries to present fairly the balances in the FS.

We recommended that Management submit to the Audit Team copies of the MOA and supporting documents on the subject fund transfers and ensure their submission for any subsequent fund transfers. Effect a fund transfer for the amount inadvertently utilized in the Trust Fund and prepare a JEV for the necessary adjustment.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasury Office (MTO) explore and implement other collection strategies to further improve revenue assessment and collection. Periodic collection performance and monitoring must be conducted to assure efficient collection of projected income.

We recommended that Management submit legal basis on the grant of honoraria or incentive otherwise stop the practice of granting  honoraria/incentive to the members of the abovementioned councils, boards and committees for their attendance to meetings/symposia and to employees performing inherent functions.

We recommended that Management prepare the JEV and record the JEV in the Check Disbursement Journal only upon receipt of the Report of Checks Issued prepared by the Municipal Treasurer in accordance with the Check Disbursement Process embodied in the Manual on NGAS for LGU.

We recommended that Management submit lacking supporting documents and copy of explanations/justifications on the deficiencies noted and henceforth, require the concerned Officials to strictly comply with the Budget Operations Manual and the Local Government Code for its local budget operations.

We recommended that Management:
Strictly adhere to the above cited provisions on the procurement of infrastructure projects.  Henceforth, all perfected contracts together with its supporting documents must be submitted for review and evaluation to the Office of the Auditor within five working days from execution of contract;
Require the contractor to post a warranty security in place of retention money for the period supposed to be covered by a warranty security before releasing the retention money to the contractor; and
Direct concerned officials to ensure that procurement for infrastructure projects are properly supported with complete documentation.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Engineer to submit the As-Built Plans prepared by the Contractor for the abovementioned fully paid completed infrastructure projects and the Municipal Accountant, henceforth, to ensure that As-Built plan is attached as one of the supporting documents before effecting final payment of completed infrastructure project.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Engineer to properly monitor the timelines of the implementation of infrastructure projects to avoid unnecessary delay, or if unavoidable, to ensure that there is an approved Request for Time Extension, Suspension Order or Variation Order to support the same. Management must also ensure that liquidated damages is imposed and paid by the contractor in case of unjustified delays.

We recommended that Management strictly comply with the requirement of the law to allocate at least 5 percent of its total estimated revenue for the year for DRRM and ensure to fully implement the projects that have been planned and duly provided with budget.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain separate SLs for each allocation on 30 percent QRF and 70 percent DRRMF-MOOE by year, ensure that all required disclosure in the Notes to FS on inventories, PPE and Special Trust Fund for LDRRMF are complied with, and that all fund transfers from various sources intended for DRRM PPAs are taken up as Trust Liability-DRRM in the Trust Fund books.

We recommended that Management require the Chairperson of GFPS and other members to ensure that the GAD budget is at least 5 percent of the LGU’s total appropriation and implement all the projects and activities programmed in the GAD Plan and Budget to attain optimum utilization of the GAD Funds for the intended beneficiaries.

We recommended that Management instruct the Municipal Accountant to:
Review and analyze the abnormal negative balances of the subsidiary accounts of Due to BIR, Due to GSIS, Due to Pag-IBIG and Due to GOCCs accounts, coordinate with the respective agencies for the reconciliation of both of their records and the possibility of claim for refund of over remittances, if any, and effect the necessary adjustments to present fairly the balances in the FS; and
Ensure that there would be no over remittance of withheld amounts in the succeeding years.

We recommended that Management replenish the said bank accounts to save them from being closed and incurring unnecessary bank charges and exercise due diligence in ensuring that there would be no loss or wastage of government funds.

We recommended that Management require the PG to immediately submit the CY 2020 and CY 2021 Fund Utilization and other reports for monitoring of the Enterprise’s operations and appropriate recording of liquidations in the PRDP books.

We recommended that Management consider requiring the Contractors to provide/post warranty securities to enforce their liabilities in case of any structural defects or failures that may arise within the remaining warranty period.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
a. Require the Municipal Accountant to work back and exert extra effort in
identifying cause/s of unreconciled difference and to prepare and regularly
submit to the Auditor bank reconciliation statements for all bank accounts for
our timely review/verification and to prepare a Journal Entry Voucher to take
up reconciling items or adjustments;
b. Require the Acting Municipal Treasurer to prepare, maintain and update
cashbooks for all the bank accounts of the LGU; and
c. Consider closing bank account/s which is/are deemed unnecessary or a
duplication of an existing account.

We recommended that Management, thru the Municipal Accountant, avail the
remedy to clean its books by requesting for an authority from the Commission on
Audit to write-off dormant receivable account balances, collectability of which are
already considered remote, and support the same with the relevant documents
pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

We recommended that Management:
a. Require the Inventory Committee to continuously make utmost effort to
conduct a complete and detailed physical inventory of all the LGU’s
properties in accordance with the new guidelines and procedures prescribed
by COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31 2020, and to prepare and
submit, within the deadlines set, the RPCPPE together with the Report on
50
the Physical Count of the Road Network System of the LGU;
b. Instruct the General Services Officer to prepare and maintain the Local
Road Inventory Map and Property Cards for each type of properties as
basis for reconciliation with the PPE accounts with the accounting records;
c. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to prepare and maintain, in relation to
the local road networks of the LGU, Local Road Network Ledger Card
(LRNLC), including the regular property ledger cards, also as basis for
reconciliation with the General Ledger account balances and with the
property records; and
d. Require the GSO and Municipal Accountant to make a final reconciliation
of their respective records and avail the one-time cleansing of PPE account
balances, strictly in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 and adjust
the books accordingly, in order to come up with a reliable PPE balances that
are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

We recommended the following to Management:
a. Require the Municipal Accountant to review and analyze recorded
Accounts Payable that remained outstanding for more than two years to
determine the validity of claims and ensure that all recorded payables are
duly supported with the required documents and only those true and valid
payables of the Municipality are reflected in the financial statements;
b. Act on the reversion of accounts payables which remained outstanding for
more than two years and are ascertained to be without valid claims,
pursuant to Section 98 of PD No. 1445; and
c. Subsidiary Leaders must be updated/maintained for all recorded Accounts
Payable for monitoring and future reference purposes.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Acting Municipal
Treasurer exert efforts to reconcile the reciprocal Due to LGUs and Due from LGUs
accounts between the Municipality and the Province and Barangays.
A journal entry voucher must be prepared to take up in the books of the
Municipality any adjustments noted during the reconciliation process.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to
exhaust all means to analyze and reconcile Assets and Liabilities accounts with
abnormal balances as of December 31, 2020 and accordingly make the necessary
adjustment/s of the affected accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer see to it that the Petty Cash fund is fully settled at the end of every year and its unutilized balance is refunded/returned to the municipal treasury and deposited to the AGDB. Strict adherence to Section 48 of the Manual on the LGU-NGAS, particularly the full liquidation of the Petty Cash account at the end of the year, is enjoined.

We recommended that Management exhaust all possible means to require the liquidation or settlement of cash advances including, but not limited to, the following:
Demand from the concerned AOs the immediate submission of liquidation documents and/or refund of the cash advance if not yet utilized;
For long outstanding cash advances, exert effort to retrieve the documents relative thereto, as basis for the issuance of demand letter by the Municipal Accountant; and
Institute appropriate criminal or administrative sanctions for the continued non-compliance with the existing laws, rules and regulations on the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances, if warranted.

We recommended the continuous and sustained effort of the Inventory Committee in conducting an annual physical inventory of all properties of the Municipality and in submitting the same within the prescribed period.
We further recommended that Management require the General Services Office and the Accounting Office to prioritize the reconciliation process and ensure that discrepancies noted are immediately investigated and adjusted in the books accordingly.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to release the barangay shares on RPT and CTC collections on a quarterly basis.
Further, since remittance of the barangay shares were significantly delayed, it is recommended to immediately remit the barangay shares for CY 2019 and prior years without any condition.

We recommended that the BPLO and the MTO:
Require the taxpayers to present and submit sworn statement of capital investment for new business registration or sworn statement of gross sales/receipts of the preceding year or quarter for renewal applications upon application for Mayor/Business Permit and/or other financial documents such as Audited FS;
Require those who have been granted with Mayor’s/Business Permit to submit a certified copy of their Annual ITR for the preceding year, or if not yet available, the ITR for the next preceding calendar year on or before April 30 of each year; and
Enforce strict implementation of Chapter II of the Revenue Code of the Municipality to generate additional business tax revenue to finance more Municipal projects.
We further recommended that the MTO must perform its “visitorial functions” and /or conduct other alternative procedures to ascertain the correctness of declared gross receipts/sales by taxpayers. Moreover, the MTO must also retain a copy of the required documents as taxpayer’s permanent file for current and future reference.

We recommended the following:
That the Municipality, through the Municipal Treasurer, strictly follow the procedural guidelines prescribed in the Revenue Code and the IRR of the LGC for the termination or retirement of business;
That the Municipality strictly implement the provision of Section 171 of the LGC to ensure the accuracy of business tax collected and penalize erring business taxpayers by requiring them to pay tax deficiencies including applicable interests and surcharges; and
Collect the taxes due from these business establishments, including any interest, surcharges or penalties for non-payment or delay in payment.

We recommended that Management require the LSB to obtain/secure the required DepEd approved SIP and DEDP and other information from the DepEd RO on the allocation of each school from the National Budget as contained in the NEP, which supposedly were the basis for the preparation and approval by the LSB of the SEF Budget and furnish the Audit Team, for reference and review purposes. Henceforth, the LSB must strictly follow the mandated required processes and documents in planning and budgeting for SEF under the revised guidelines on the use of SEF.

We recommended that Management require the LDRRM Officer to submit the monthly Report on the Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF on or before the 15th day after the end of each month pursuant to the aforecited Circular, to facilitate timely verification and audit thereof.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer to cause the transfer of the amount of ₱14.33 million representing the unexpended balances of LDRRMF for CYs 2018 and 2019 to the special trust fund account.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant draw a Journal Entry Voucher upon transfer of the fund to correct the understatement by the same amount of both the Transfers of Unspent Current Year DRRM Funds to the Trust Funds and Trust Liability-DRRMF accounts.

We recommended that:
The MBO, MDRRMO and Municipal Accounting Office work out on tracing the reconciling items, establish their existence/non-existence and determine from there any further action that may be undertaken. Also, ensure that reconciliation of the unexpended balance of current year and continuing appropriation of DRRM Funds and the unexpended DRRM Fund lodged in the Trust Fund at year end be made for the ensuing years; and
The Municipal Accountant, in drawing the disbursement voucher to effect the transfer of the unexpended balances of QRF and Mitigating Fund-MOOE to the Trust Fund bank account, must properly validate and verify the correctness of the reported unexpended balance by the MBO to ensure that there would be no excess or insufficiency of fund transfer.

We recommended that Management maximize the utilization of the LDRRMF based on the approved Annual Investment Plan to support disaster risk management activities following the policies and procedures on the utilization of the LDRRMF pursuant to paragraph 6.0 of NDRRMC, DBM and DILG JMC No. 2013-1 dated March 25, 2013.

We recommended the continuous and sustained effort of GFPS in their adherence to provisions of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01, as amended by PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2016-01 and COA Circular No. 2014-001. Furthermore, submit a copy of the Annual GAD Plan and Budget and the corresponding Accomplishment Report to COA within the prescribed period for the timely audit so that recommended corrective measures may also be issued in the appropriate time.

We recommended that Management strictly comply with the law and applicable rules and regulations on the payment of honoraria to the Members of the BAC, BAC Secretariat and TWG, particularly the sources of fund, the rates allowed for each successfully completed procurement project, the limit of 25 per cent of the basic monthly salary, and its payment only after each procurement project is already considered as successfully completed.
In appropriating and using savings from the local budgets as one of the sources of fund for BAC honoraria, Management must strictly comply with the pertinent provisions of RA No. 7160 and items 3.2 and 3.3 of DBM Budget Circular No. 2007-3, as existence of appropriation for BAC honoraria without complying with the budgetary rules and regulations will not validate payment thereof.
Consequently, the BAC Members must immediately refund the excess amount of ₱0.56 million subject of Notice of Disallowance No. 20-001-(19) dated February 5, 2020.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Identify specific causes of delays in the subproject implementation, address the existing condition, deviations and deficiencies noted;
Submit an approved revised Implementation and Procurement Plans together with the relevant documents for validation of the Audit Team, if applicable; and
Call the attention of the Municipal Project Management and Implementation Unit (MPMIU) to ensure the timely execution of the Work and Financial Plan.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Disburse funds and conduct procurement procedures strictly in accordance with the PIPs, work and financial plan timelines; and
Fast track the disbursement process while ensuring complete and adequate supporting documents to facilitate submission of liquidation to DA.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Submit the financial statements and other accounting and project reports that are already due for submission;
Identify and resolve the causes of delay and non-submission of the mandatory financial statements and project reports with complete supporting documents/schedules within the prescribed period, otherwise, institute appropriate sanctions against the responsible officers for the continued non-compliance with existing regulations; and
Ensure that financial documents and reports are properly accomplished and all supporting schedules are attached to the report.

We recommended that Management immediately submit to the Audit Team copies of perfected contracts/POs with complete supporting documents relative to the implementation of then PRDP subprojects for a timely and complete conduct of its auditorial and technical review and to eventually preclude audit suspensions.

We recommended that the Head of the Procuring Entity (HoPE) and BAC conduct review and investigation whether the provision of conflict of interest was not violated. An explanation is also requested why the BAC allowed the bidder brothers to participate in the public bidding knowing that they may undermine the real and strict competitiveness of the bidding.

We recommended that the LGU Aroroy enforce strictly the provisions of the procurement law as well as the laws on government contract. We request for an explanation why the contract was still awarded to ARKY Construction and Supply despite its inability to enter into a contract with the government within the 10-day period as required by law and contemplated in the Notice of Award (NOA).

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, in approving disbursements, strictly conform with the approved IMA and the mandated financing mix of the PRDP, otherwise, submit a revised IMA and its schedules to show that the changes made were in conformity thereto.

We recommended that Management require the project Accountant to reconcile the discrepancies between the cash accounts reported in SSAF/SRE and Statement of Financial Position/General Ledger. Thereafter, effect the necessary adjustments on the omissions/errors to correct the reported balances of the affected accounts/balances in the books and SSA/SRE, as applicable.

We recommended that the Management direct the Municipal Accountant to determine, once and for all, the collectability of these Receivable accounts together with the dormant cash advances and to document the request for write-off from the Commission on Audit the account balances which were considered dormant and could no longer be collected pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.
Accordingly, request authority from the Commission on Audit to write-off the dormant accounts duly supported with the required documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management refrain those collectors who practice lapping of collections and who incurred delays in their remittances, from acting as revenue collectors. Replace them with trustworthy personnel having high virtue on accountability for better fiscal control in the treasury. For those erring accountable officers, the Management must consider instituting appropriate administrative and/or criminal action against them. The Municipal Treasurer must require all collectors to prepare RCDs and remit all collections at the close of each business day. In cases when she is on travel or other official business outside office and could not act as such, she must not allow any collectors to take charge of the latter’s office and act on her behalf.

We recommended that the Accountable Officer explain the difference/variance noted in the analysis of accountable forms and produce the collections or present the validated deposit slip/s if these cash tickets were entirely issued. We also recommended evaluation by Management, particularly the Office of the Municipal Treasurer, of the existing controls, identification of weakness or any deviation from the standard internal control measures in handling accountable forms and undertaking steps to improve and/or correct them.

We reiterated our previous recommendation that the Management:
Demand immediate settlement/liquidation of the outstanding cash advances of the Municipal Treasurer pursuant to COA Circular No. 97-002; and,
Require the Municipal Accountant to strictly monitor the grant and liquidation of cash advances and avoid granting additional CAs unless the previous cash advance is first settled or a proper accounting thereof is made pursuant to Section 89 of PD No. 1445.

We recommended that the Management require:
The General Services Office (i) to secure insurance of all its insurable property from the GSIS in order to mitigate possible losses on the part of the Municipality; and (ii) to submit an inventory list of insurable properties; and
The Budget Office to ensure that appropriate amount is included in the budget to cover for the property insurance premiums.

We recommended that-
The Municipal Treasury Office and the Budget Office as the lead offices-
Conduct a systematic and realistic forecasting of revenue taking into account prior years’ experience and statistics as aide in setting targets;
Review the present budgeting to set a clearer guide for the revenue generating Departments towards attaining income projections; and
Provide for the targets on income accounts that did not have projections which must be based on statistics, prior years’ experience and future expectation.
Periodic review must be made by concerned municipal officials to ascertain whether collection targets are attained.  The causes for the shortfall in collections must be analyzed and alternative measures must be looked into to avoid incurrence of misses in projections in the future; and
The Municipal Treasurer must conduct periodic review to check the collection performance level and devise remedial measures for revenue accounts that missed the projections and effective procedures that will improve more the collections of taxes that registered good results.

We recommended that-
The Municipal Treasury Office implement collection strategies to further improve revenue assessment and collection. Periodic collection performance and monitoring must be conducted to assure efficient collection of projected income;
The Municipal Treasurer or her authorized representative examine the books, accounts, and other pertinent records of any person, partnership, corporation, or association subject to local taxes, fees and charges in order to ascertain. assess, and collect the correct amount of the tax, fee, or charge pursuant to Section 171 of Local government Code; and
The Local Finance Committee or the Municipal Treasurer, Budget Officer and Municipal Planning Officer formulate realistic income projections based on Statistics of past years’ experience and profile of local taxpayers and devise effective plan to avoid, mitigate and control the risks of possible non- collection of projected incomes.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant establish and maintain special accounts, complete subsidiary ledgers, books of accounts and prepare separate financial statements and/or financial reports to account for the operations of each economic enterprise for the consumption of the LGU and other users of these information to assess the performance of each LEE and for decision making directed towards financial self-sufficiency.

We recommended that Management:
Require all drivers and concerned officials to use the prescribed forms and to completely fill up the driver’s trip ticket in accordance with existing regulation for transparency and accountability over the use of such government motor vehicles;
Prepare and submit Monthly Report of Official Travels and Monthly Fuel Consumption Report of LGU vehicles and heavy equipment to the Commission on Audit (thru the Municipal Accountant) for verification and evaluation of the Auditor within 10 days of the ensuing month; and,
Establish and implement effective control over the use of government vehicle and fuel, oil and lubricants consumption to prevent wasteful, excessive and unnecessary expenditures.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor stop approving the payment of honoraria to the members of the abovementioned councils, boards and committees unless sufficient legal basis for the grant of said honorarium has been submitted to the Audit Team for further evaluation of the matter on hand. Otherwise, the payments amounting to ₱1.91 million during CY 2018 and subsequent payments may be considered for further audit action as audit suspension or disallowance.

We recommended that the Management limit the payment of honoraria to the Members of the BAC within the available funds arising from authorized sources and ensure that they are paid the same for each procurement project which are already considered as successfully completed. Consequently, the BAC Members must immediately refund the excess amount of ₱1.84 million they have received during CY 2018.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor stop approving the payment of honoraria to the LGU officials and employees who are designated as managers of Public Employment Services Office, Fish Port and Terminal Complex operation, El Oro Radyo Station, and Coastal Resource Management Program, unless there be sufficient legal basis presented to the Audit Team for evaluation. Otherwise, the payments amounting to ₱527,436 during CY 2018 and subsequent payments may be considered for further audit action as audit suspension or disallowance. If, indeed, the legal basis and other documents to be presented show that these are special projects under DBM BC No. 2007-2, the honorarium must be paid in accordance with the computation set forth therein.

We recommended that in the conduct of seminars/trainings/workshops and the like, whether in-house or in other agencies outside of the sponsoring agency, the LGU, through the officials and employees of the concerned offices in charge thereof, must strictly comply with the guidelines set forth in DBM Circular No. 2007-1 and COA Circular No 2012-001 in the payment of honoraria.

We recommended that the Management require the Accounting Office to ensure that withholding taxes, as applicable, are deducted from payments of allowable honoraria in accordance with the Revised Withholding Tax Table and the same are remitted to the BIR within the prescribed period.

We recommended to the Management to require the Bids and Awards Committee to prepare and submit to the GPPB the procurement monitoring report within the time frame as stipulated in Section 12.2 of the Revised IRR of RA 9184, so that monitoring and evaluation of the LGUs compliance to the provisions of the Procurement Act can be made. We recommended that the Municipality ensure that their 10-year Solid Waste Management Plan be approved by the NSWC, through continuous coordination and communication with the said agency, so that not only will the planned programs, projects, or activities be in harmony with the National Solid Waste Management Framework but also to ensure that total protection of the environment and the general welfare of its constituents in harmony with economic development will be attained.
